message.text: The 'userName' parameter from the login request is passed directly into MongoDB's findOne() query without sanitization. An attacker can submit a JSON object such as {"$gt": ""} as the userName field, causing the query to match unintended documents and bypass authentication.
Passwords are stored in plaintext in MongoDB. The bcrypt hashing code in addUser (line 29) is commented out. If the database is compromised, all user credentials are immediately exposed.

The /learn route uses req.query.url directly in res.redirect() without any validation or allowlist check. An attacker can craft a link such as /learn?url=https://evil.com to redirect authenticated users to a phishing or malware site.
Password comparison uses direct string equality (fromDB === fromUser) instead of bcrypt.compareSync. This confirms the authentication system relies entirely on plaintext passwords.

The bcrypt-nodejs package is deprecated and unmaintained. Even if hashing were enabled, using this library introduces supply-chain risk and potential cryptographic weaknesses. Migrate to 'bcrypt' or 'bcryptjs'.

The addUser function (line 17) accepts userName, firstName, lastName, password, and email from untrusted request input without any type, length, or format validation. This enables injection of arbitrary data types into MongoDB documents.
POST /signup route is registered without CSRF protection middleware. An attacker can forge cross-site signup requests to create accounts or exhaust resources.

SSN and date-of-birth are stored and retrieved in plaintext. The encryption/decryption helpers in profile-dao.js are commented out, leaving sensitive PII unprotected in the database. An attacker who gains read access to the database (e.g. via NoSQL injection in another endpoint) obtains raw SSN and DOB values.

The regex /([0-9]+)+\#/ used to validate bankRouting contains nested quantifiers ('+' inside a group followed by '+'). This causes catastrophic backtracking on crafted input such as '000000000000000000000000000!' — an attacker can send this value to exhaust CPU and cause denial of service.

The user-supplied 'website' field is encoded with ESAPI encodeForHTML(), but the value is also used as a URL in an anchor href attribute. HTML-entity encoding does not prevent javascript: URI schemes or other URL-context attacks. The correct mitigation is encodeForURL() as noted in the source comment.

The website field is encoded using encodeForHTML() but is used in the context of a URL href attribute. This is the wrong encoding context — encodeForURL() should be used instead. An attacker can craft a javascript: URI that bypasses HTML encoding but executes when clicked as a link.

Server-Side JavaScript Injection: req.body.preTax is passed directly to eval(). An attacker can submit arbitrary JavaScript (e.g., 'require("child_process").exec("rm -rf /")') as the preTax field value to achieve Remote Code Execution on the server.

The contributions-dao.js update() method passes user-controlled values (preTax, afterTax, roth) directly into a MongoDB document without type validation or sanitization at the DAO layer. While the route handler calls eval() (a separate, more severe issue), if eval returns an object instead of a number, it could manipulate the MongoDB document structure. The isNaN check occurs after eval, but crafted payloads can pass isNaN while still being objects with valueOf().

In contributions-dao.js update(), the MongoDB query filter uses the raw userId ({userId}) while the document stores parseInt(userId) as parsedUserId. This type mismatch means the upsert query filter and the stored userId field may differ in type (string vs integer), potentially causing duplicate records or query mismatches that could be exploited to read or modify another user's contributions.

The 'threshold' query parameter is interpolated directly into a MongoDB $where clause without sanitization. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript that MongoDB's SpiderMonkey engine will execute server-side. For example, supplying threshold=0';while(true){}' causes a denial-of-service, and threshold=1'; return 1 == '1 bypasses the filter to return all records. The commented-out fix (parseInt + range check) is not active.

The userId for allocation lookup is taken from req.params.userId (the URL path) instead of req.session.userId. Any authenticated user can enumerate and access other users' allocation data by changing the userId in the URL path (e.g., GET /allocations/2). The commented-out fix that reads userId from the session is not active.

Stored XSS: User-supplied memo content from req.body.memo is stored in MongoDB without any sanitization, then rendered via marked() in the Swig template with autoescape disabled. An attacker can submit a memo containing malicious JavaScript (e.g., `[clickme](javascript:alert('XSS'))` or HTML injection payloads that bypass marked 0.3.5's sanitize option) that executes in every user's browser when viewing the memos page.

marked 0.3.5 has multiple known XSS vulnerabilities (CVE-2017-17461, CVE-2022-21680, CVE-2022-21681). The sanitize option used in server.js (line 127) is deprecated and trivially bypassable in this version. Attackers can craft markdown payloads that bypass the sanitize filter and inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript.

Swig template engine autoescape is explicitly disabled (autoescape: false on line 137). This means all template variables including marked() output are rendered as raw HTML without escaping. Combined with the stored memo content, this creates a direct stored XSS vector affecting all users who view the memos page.

The memo content from req.body.memo is passed directly to memosDAO.insert() without any input validation, length checks, or content sanitization. There is no server-side check on the type, length, or content of the memo field before it is stored in the database.

req.body.memo is passed directly into a MongoDB insert operation. Since bodyParser.urlencoded with extended:false is used, the memo field is a string and direct NoSQL operator injection via this field is limited. However, if the content type were changed to JSON (bodyParser.json is also enabled), an attacker could send an object instead of a string for the memo field, potentially causing unexpected document structures in the database.

The GET /benefits and POST /benefits routes are protected only by isLoggedIn middleware, but lack the isAdmin check. Any authenticated user can access admin-only benefits management functionality. The fix (isAdmin middleware) is present in the source code but commented out on lines 58-59.

The POST /benefits handler extracts userId and benefitStartDate directly from req.body without authorization checks (any authenticated user can modify any other user's benefits). The userId is passed to parseInt() then used as a MongoDB _id filter, and benefitStartDate is written directly to the database without sanitization or format validation. Combined with the missing isAdmin check, this allows any authenticated user to modify arbitrary users' benefit records.

Server-Side Request Forgery via user-controlled URL. The `req.query.url` parameter is directly concatenated with `req.query.symbol` and passed to `needle.get()` without any validation or allowlisting. An attacker can supply an arbitrary URL (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ for cloud metadata, or internal service URLs) to make the server issue requests to internal or external resources on the attacker's behalf.

Reflected Cross-Site Scripting via unescaped response body. The response body from the external `needle.get()` request is written directly to the HTTP response via `res.write(body)` with Content-Type `text/html` and no output encoding or sanitization. If the attacker controls the target URL (via the SSRF above) or the remote server returns malicious HTML/JavaScript, it will be rendered in the victim's browser. This is a second-order XSS chained with the SSRF.

The tutorial router is mounted at /tutorial without the isLoggedIn middleware, unlike most other routes in the application. While the tutorial pages use a hardcoded whitelist of page names (a1-a10, redos, ssrf) which prevents path traversal, the lack of authentication means tutorial content is accessible to unauthenticated users. This is likely intentional but should be confirmed, as tutorial pages may reference internal application details useful for reconnaissance.

Password is stored as plaintext. The bcrypt hashing on line 29 is commented out, so the raw password from the request is written directly to MongoDB. Any database compromise yields all user credentials in cleartext.

The userName parameter is passed directly into MongoDB findOne query without type checking. If the HTTP layer passes a parsed JSON object (e.g. {"$gt": ""}), the attacker can bypass authentication entirely by matching any user document.

Plaintext passwords ('Admin_123', 'User1_123', 'User2_123') are hardcoded in the database seed script and committed to version control. The bcrypt hashed alternatives are commented out. These default credentials are trivially discoverable and may persist in production.

updateUser accepts a userId parameter and uses it directly in the MongoDB update query with no authorization check at the DAO layer. If the route handler does not verify that the authenticated session user matches the target userId, any authenticated user can modify another user's profile including SSN, bank account, and routing number.

The file imports bcrypt-nodejs which has been deprecated since 2017 and is no longer maintained. Even if the commented-out hashing code were re-enabled, it would rely on an unmaintained library with potential security issues. Replace with 'bcrypt' or 'bcryptjs'.

Plaintext passwords are hardcoded in the database seed script for admin ('Admin_123'), user1 ('User1_123'), and user2 ('User2_123'). The commented-out bcrypt hashes (lines 19, 28, 33) indicate awareness of proper password storage but the active code uses plaintext. If this seed is run in any non-ephemeral environment, accounts are trivially compromised.

In the update method, callback(null, allocations) is called at line 49 inside the success branch, but callback(err, null) at line 53 executes unconditionally after the if-block. On success (err is falsy), both callbacks fire: first the async one from getUserById (line 49), then the synchronous one at line 53 with (undefined, null). This double-callback can cause response headers to be sent twice, leading to crashes or inconsistent state.

The 'memo' parameter is inserted into MongoDB without any input sanitization or output encoding. Since memos are rendered as HTML via the marked library (as indicated by the surface description), an attacker can store arbitrary JavaScript in the memo field. When other users view the memo, the script executes in their browser context, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, or defacement.

The 'memo' parameter is embedded directly into the MongoDB document without type checking or validation. If the caller passes a user-controlled object (e.g., from req.body) without ensuring it is a string, an attacker could inject MongoDB operators or manipulate the document structure. The lack of schema validation at the DAO layer means arbitrary fields or nested objects could be persisted.

needle 2.2.4 is affected by CVE-2020-7669 (prototype pollution via crafted response headers) and related vulnerabilities. If an attacker controls the target server (possible via the SSRF), malicious response headers can pollute Object.prototype, potentially leading to denial of service or remote code execution depending on downstream usage.

The session secret is a hardcoded plaintext string 'session_cookie_secret_key_here' in config/env/all.js. This weak, predictable secret allows an attacker to forge session cookies and hijack any user session. The secret should be a high-entropy value loaded from an environment variable.

The express-session configuration does not set the httpOnly cookie flag. Without httpOnly, session cookies are accessible to client-side JavaScript, enabling session theft via XSS attacks. The fix (cookie: { httpOnly: true }) is present in a comment block but not active.

The express-session configuration does not set the secure cookie flag. Without secure: true, session cookies are transmitted over plaintext HTTP, allowing interception via network sniffing (session hijacking). The fix is commented out in server.js line 98.

The session cookie has no maxAge or expires set. Sessions persist indefinitely until the browser is closed (or not at all if the session store persists). This increases the window for session fixation and replay attacks. A TODO on line 96 acknowledges the missing maxAge.

The session uses the default cookie name 'connect.sid', which reveals the application is built on Express/Connect. An attacker can use this to fingerprint the framework and target known vulnerabilities. The fix (key: 'sessionId') is commented out on line 89.

The application is served over plain HTTP (http.createServer on line 145). All traffic including session cookies is transmitted in cleartext. The HTTPS server setup exists but is commented out (lines 149-155). Without HTTPS, even adding the secure cookie flag would break sessions rather than protect them.

The cookie session secret is hardcoded as 'session_cookie_secret_key_here'. This default value is predictable and allows an attacker to forge session cookies, leading to session hijacking. The secret should be loaded from an environment variable with no insecure default.

The cryptographic key is hardcoded as 'a_secure_key_for_crypto_here'. Any attacker who reads the source code (or the published npm package) can decrypt all data encrypted with this key. The key must be loaded from an external secret store or environment variable.

The cryptographic algorithm is set to 'aes256' (AES-256-CBC by default in Node.js crypto). CBC mode without authenticated encryption (e.g. AES-256-GCM) is vulnerable to padding oracle attacks. Consider using 'aes-256-gcm' for authenticated encryption.

The entire configuration object (including cookieSecret, cryptoKey, and database URI) is logged to the console at startup via console.log and util.inspect. This exposes secrets in log files, CI output, and container logs. Remove or redact sensitive fields before logging.

The ZAP API key is hardcoded as 'v9dn0balpqas1pcc281tn5ood1' in the development configuration. Although this is for a testing tool, hardcoded API keys in source code can be accidentally used in production or leak through version control.
The ZAP API key is hardcoded as 'v9dn0balpqas1pcc281tn5ood1' in the test configuration. Same key reused across environments increases blast radius if compromised.

The development configuration injects an inline script via document.write that loads an external script from a dynamically constructed URL. If an attacker controls the host header or DNS, they can inject arbitrary JavaScript. The livereload script also uses an unencrypted HTTP connection.

The login handler assigns req.session.userId without calling req.session.regenerate() first. An attacker who sets a known session ID (via cookie injection or URL fixation) before the victim logs in can hijack the authenticated session, since the session ID persists unchanged across the authentication boundary. The code comments at lines 104-115 explicitly acknowledge this vulnerability and show the fix (wrapping in req.session.regenerate()) but it is not applied.

Swig template engine autoescape is explicitly disabled (autoescape: false). This globally disables HTML entity encoding for all template variables rendered with {{ }}, exposing every view to reflected and stored XSS. The commented-out fix confirms this is intentional for demonstration but represents a critical misconfiguration.

Reflected XSS in profile page. Multiple user-controlled fields (lastName, ssn, dob, bankAcc, bankRouting, address, website, updateError) are rendered unescaped in the profile template via {{ variable }} with autoescape disabled. An attacker who controls these database values (e.g., via profile update) can inject scripts that execute when the profile page is rendered.

Reflected XSS in login page. The loginError and userName variables are rendered unescaped via {{loginError}} and {{userName}}. If the server reflects user input (e.g., a bad username) into these variables without sanitization, an attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript.

Reflected XSS in signup page. Multiple error messages and form re-population values (userNameError, firstNameError, lastNameError, passwordError, verifyError, emailError, userName, firstName, lastName) are rendered without escaping. If the server echoes user input in these variables, script injection is possible.

Stored XSS in layout navigation bar. The {{firstName}} and {{lastName}} variables are rendered unescaped in the layout template's navigation header. Since layout.html is the base template for all authenticated pages, a user who sets their name to a malicious script payload will have it executed on every page load for their session.

Stored XSS in benefits admin page. User-controlled fields (user.firstName, user.lastName, user.benefitStartDate) are rendered unescaped in the benefits table. A user who sets their name to a malicious payload will have it execute in the admin's browser when they view the benefits page.

Stored XSS in allocations page. The {{allocation.firstName}} and {{allocation.lastName}} values are rendered without escaping. An attacker who modifies their profile name to include script tags will have the payload execute when any user views the allocations page.

Reflected XSS in error template. The {{error}} variable is rendered without escaping in error-template.html. If the error message contains user-controlled input (e.g., from a URL path or parameter that triggers an error), an attacker can inject arbitrary scripts.

Swig 1.4.2 is an unmaintained template engine (last updated 2014, archived). It has known security issues and receives no patches. The project should migrate to a maintained template engine such as Nunjucks, EJS, or Handlebars.

MongoDB connection string 'mongodb://localhost:27017/nodegoat' uses no authentication. If the MONGODB_URI environment variable is unset, the application connects to MongoDB without any credentials. An attacker with network access to port 27017 can read, modify, or delete all data in the database.

MongoClient.connect() is called with the connection string from config without validating that authentication credentials are present. The driver does not enforce authentication by default, so a missing username/password in the URI results in an unauthenticated session.

Session cookie secret is hardcoded as 'session_cookie_secret_key_here'. This value is predictable and shared across all deployments, allowing an attacker to forge session cookies and hijack any user's session.

Cryptographic key is hardcoded as 'a_secure_key_for_crypto_here' and used with AES-256. A hardcoded key provides no confidentiality: anyone with source code access can decrypt all data protected by this key.

The server uses http.createServer() instead of https.createServer(). All traffic including session cookies, credentials, and user data is transmitted in cleartext, vulnerable to network sniffing and man-in-the-middle attacks. The HTTPS implementation exists in comments but is disabled.

Security headers (helmet frameguard, noCache, CSP, HSTS, nosniff) and CSRF protection are commented out. The application is missing critical HTTP security headers, leaving it vulnerable to clickjacking, XSS, MIME-sniffing, and cross-site request forgery attacks.
